flashcards.front: What is the Heapsort algorithm and how does it work?
flashcards.back: Heapsort is a comparison-based sorting algorithm that uses a binary heap data structure. It works in two phases: first building a max-heap from the array, then repeatedly extracting the maximum element (root) and heapifying the remaining heap until all elements are sorted. Time complexity: O(n log n) worst-case, space: O(1).

flashcards.front: Explain the heapify operation in Heapsort.
flashcards.back: Heapify (or max-heapify) is a procedure that maintains the max-heap property by comparing a parent with its children and swapping if a child is larger. Starting from a given node, it recursively checks and swaps with the larger child until the subtree satisfies the heap property. This operation takes O(log n) time for a heap of size n.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity analysis of Heapsort?
flashcards.back: Building the max-heap takes O(n) time. Then, we perform n extractions each requiring O(log n) time, giving O(n log n). Overall: O(n log n) in worst, average, and best cases — making it time-optimal. The space complexity is O(1) as it sorts in-place.

Randomized QuickSort is a variant of QuickSort that randomly selects the pivot element (usually by choosing a random index) instead of using a fixed position like the last element. This random selection ensures that the expected partition sizes are balanced, reducing the probability of worst-case behavior and improving average-case performance.

flashcards.front: What is probabilistic analysis in randomized algorithms?
flashcards.back: Probabilistic analysis is a technique to analyze algorithms by considering the expected performance over all possible inputs or random choices made by the algorithm. For Randomized QuickSort, it involves calculating the expected number of comparisons by considering all possible pivot sequences and their probabilities, yielding an average-case complexity of O(n log n).

Randomized Selection (QuickSelect) finds the k-th smallest element in an unordered list. It works by randomly partitioning the array (like QuickSort), then recursively searching only in the partition containing the k-th element. Expected time: O(n), worst-case: O(n²), but randomization makes worst-case unlikely.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Monte Carlo and Las Vegas algorithms?
flashcards.back: Monte Carlo algorithms always produce a result but may occasionally be incorrect (bounded error), while Las Vegas algorithms never produce an incorrect result but their running time may vary randomly. Example: Randomized QuickSort is Las Vegas (correct result, variable time), while QuickSelect is Monte Carlo (fast but may occasionally select wrong element in modified versions).

flashcards.front: Compare Expected vs Worst-case time complexity for Randomized QuickSort.
flashcards.back: For Randomized QuickSort: Expected time complexity is O(n log n), much better than deterministic QuickSort's O(n²) worst-case. The randomization ensures that no specific input triggers worst-case; the probability of O(n²) behavior becomes negligibly small. The expected number of comparisons is approximately 2n ln n, close to optimal comparison-based sorting.
